en.extract:
The white-fronted falconet (Microhierax latifrons), also called the Bornean falconet, is a species of bird of prey in the family Falconidae. Described by the British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1879, it is endemic to Borneo, where it is found in Sarawak, Sabah, and extreme northeastern Kalimantan. An extremely small falcon, it is only 14–17 cm (5.5–6.7 in) long and weighs 35–65 g (1.2–2.3 oz), being the smallest species of falconet. It has glossy bluish-black upperparts, black flanks and outer thighs, and a black mask. The belly and vent are pale yellowish-brown, while the throat, cheeks, and breast are white. The species can be told apart from other falconets by the colour of its crown and forehead, which is white in males and reddish-brown in females. It has no subspecies.
The species inhabits open forests, forest edges, clearings with dead trees, and cultivated regions with interspersed trees, and rarely in mangrove forests. It mainly eats insects, although it has also been reported hunting birds. The breeding season lasts from March to either June or April, with clutches of two eggs being laid in old barbet or woodpecker nest holes. It is classified as being near threatened by the International Union for Conservation of Nature and is also listed on Appendix II of CITES. Threats to the species include deforestation, forest fires, and the cage-bird trade.


Taxonomy and systematics
The white-fronted falconet was first described as Microhierax latifrons by the British ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe in 1879 on the basis of specimens from the Lawas River and Lumbidan in Borneo. The generic name Microhierax is from the Ancient Greek mikros, meaning small, and hierax, meaning hawk. The specific name latifrons is from the Latin latus, meaning wide or broad, and frons, meaning forehead. White-fronted falconet is the official common name designated by the International Ornithologists' Union.
The white-fronted falconet is one of five species in the falconet genus Microhierax, which is found in Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Philippines. A 2004 study of mitochondrial and nuclear DNA by Carole Griffiths and colleagues found that within the family Falconidae, the falconets were mostly closely related to the pygmy falcons (Polihierax). These two genera are further sister to the falcons of the genus Falco. No subspecies of the white-fronted falconet are recognised.


Description

The white-fronted falconet is an extremely small shrike-like species of falcon, with a length of 14–17 cm (5.5–6.7 in), a mass of 35–65 g (1.2–2.3 oz), and a wingspan of 28–31 cm (11–12 in). It is similar to other falconets, but is the smallest by a small amount. Both sexes have glossy bluish-black upperparts, black flanks and outer thighs, and a black mask. The throat, cheeks, and breast are white, changing to pale yellowish-brown on the belly and vent. The tail is short and black, with the wings reaching halfway to its tip. Males have a white forecrown and forehead, while females have this region reddish-brown. Females are also 9 per cent larger in size and have 4 per cent longer tails. In both sexes, the eyes are brown and the cere and legs are dark grey to black. Juveniles are similar to females, but have tawny-buff forecrowns and cheeks, while young females may also show reddish-brown on the forecrown.
The species may be confused with the black-thighed falconet, with which its range overlaps, but can be told apart from the latter and all other falconets by the colour of its forehead. The black-thighed falconet also has more intense reddish-brown on the belly and vent, along with white bars on the underside of the tail, which is pure black in the white-fronted falconet.
The white-fronted falconet's vocalizations are not well-known, but are assumed to be similar to those of other falconets. It makes a quavering kree-kree-kree.


Distribution and habitat
The white-fronted falconet is endemic to Borneo, where it is found in the Malaysian states of Sarawak and Sabah, along with the extreme northeastern part of Kalimantan (the Indonesian portion of Borneo). It inhabits open forests, forest edges, clearings with dead trees, and cultivated regions with interspersed trees, and is thought to be especially common on mountainous slopes. It is also infrequently found in mangrove forests. The species is found at elevations of 0–1,200 m (0–3,937 ft).


Behaviour and ecology
The white-fronted falconet is found alone or in groups of several individuals. The generation length in the species is 3.2 years.
The species mainly feeds on insects such as dragonflies, bees, and cicadas, but has also been reported chasing birds. Hunting parties have been observed in November. The species have been observed hunting from perches, making flights to catch flying insects and then eating the prey around two minutes after returning to the perch. Other hunting behaviours are thought to be similar to those of other falconets, although there may be differences in the hunting techniques of the present species and the black-thighed falconet.
The breeding season of the white-fronted falconet is thought to be either from March to June or from March to April, although breeding has also been observed in November. In Sabah, a male was seen feeding a fully-fledged chick in late July. Nesting occurs in old barbet or woodpecker nest holes, with clutches comprising two eggs. The incubation and fledging times are unknown. Adult males have been observed preening each other, but it is not known if it breeds communally like the collared falconet.


Status
The white-fronted falconet is classified as being near threatened by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) on the IUCN Red List, due to its small range, a moderately small population that is declining, and habitat loss within its range. It is also listed on Appendix II of CITES. The species' total population is estimated to be 15,000–30,000 total individuals, out of which 10,000–19,999 are adults. The population is estimated to be declining at a rate of 10–19% over a decade. Extensive deforestation in lowlands throughout its range, along with forest fires, is a threat, although the presence of the species in areas of secondary growth shows a degree of resistance may imply that it is not immediately threatened. The cage-bird trade may also pose a threat to the species.


References

The '''white-fronted falconet''' ('''''Microhierax latifrons'''''), also called the '''Bornean falconet''',<ref name=":3" /> is a [[species]] of [[bird of prey]] in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Falconidae]]. [[Species description|Described]] by the British [[ornithologist]] [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1879, it is [[endemism|endemic]] to [[Borneo]], where it is found in [[Sarawak]], [[Sabah]], and extreme northeastern [[Kalimantan]]. An extremely small falcon, it is only {{Convert|14–17|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{Convert|35–65|g|oz|abbr=on}}, being the smallest species of [[Microhierax|falconet]]. It has glossy bluish-black {{Birdgloss|upperparts}}, black {{Birdgloss|flanks}} and outer thighs, and a black mask. The belly and {{Birdgloss|vent}} are pale yellowish-brown, while the throat, cheeks, and breast are white. The species can be told apart from other falconets by the colour of its {{Birdgloss|crown}} and forehead, which is white in males and reddish-brown in females. It has no [[subspecies]].

The species inhabits open forests, [[forest edge]]s, clearings with dead trees, and cultivated regions with interspersed trees, and rarely in [[mangrove forest]]s. It mainly eats insects, although it has also been reported hunting birds. The breeding season lasts from March to either June or April, with [[Clutch (eggs)|clutches]] of two eggs being laid in old [[Megalaimidae|barbet]] or [[woodpecker]] nest holes. It is classified as being [[Near-threatened species|near threatened]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] and is also listed on [[Appendix II]] of [[CITES]]. Threats to the species include [[deforestation]], [[forest fires]], and the [[cage-bird trade]].

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The white-fronted falconet was first [[Species description|described]] as ''Microhierax latifrons'' by the British [[ornithologist]] [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] in 1879 on the basis of specimens from the [[Lawas River (Malaysia)|Lawas River]] and Lumbidan in [[Borneo]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Sharpe|first=Rickard Bowdler|year=1879|title=Contributions to the Ornithology of Borneo. Part IV. On the Birds of the Province of Lumbidan, North-western Borneo|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8609405#page/279/mode/1up|journal=[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]]|language=en|location=London|publisher=[[Academic Press]]|volume=4|issue=3|page=237|via=[[Biodiversity Heritage Library]]}}</ref> The [[Generic name (biology)|generic name]] ''Microhierax'' is from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''mikros'', meaning small, and ''hierax'', meaning [[hawk]]. The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''latifrons'' is from the [[Latin]] ''latus'', meaning wide or broad, and ''frons'', meaning forehead.''<ref>{{Cite book|last=Jobling|first=James A.|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names|publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]]|year=2010|isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4|location=London|pages=220, 253|language=en}}</ref>'' White-fronted falconet is the official common name designated by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|editor-last=Gill|editor-first=Frank|editor2-last=Donsker|editor2-first=David|editor3-last=Rasmussen|editor3-first=Pamela|title=Seriemas, falcons|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/falcons/|access-date=11 February 2022|website=IOC World Bird List}}</ref>

The white-fronted falconet is one of five species in the falconet [[genus]] ''[[Microhierax]]'', which is found in Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Philippines.<ref name=":0" /> A 2004 study of [[Mitochondrial DNA|mitochondrial]] and [[nuclear DNA]] by Carole Griffiths and colleagues found that within the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Falconidae]], the falconets were mostly closely related to the [[pygmy falcon]]s (''Polihierax''). These two genera are further [[Sister group|sister]] to the falcons of the genus ''[[Falco (bird)|Falco]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Griffiths|first1=Carole S.|last2=Barrowclough|first2=George F.|last3=Groth|first3=Jeff G.|last4=Mertz|first4=Lisa|date=2004|title=Phylogeny of the Falconidae (Aves): a comparison of the efficacy of morphological, mitochondrial, and nuclear data|url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S105579030300441X|journal=[[Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution]]|language=en|volume=32|issue=1|pages=101–109|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2003.11.019|pmid=15186800|bibcode=2004MolPE..32..101G |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription}}</ref> No [[subspecies]] of the white-fronted falconet are recognised.<ref name=":0" />

== Description ==
[[File:Microhierax latifrons 308678488 (cropped).jpg|alt=photo of two small black and white falcons|left|thumb|Male white-fronted falconets]]
The white-fronted falconet is an extremely small shrike-like species of falcon, with a length of {{Convert|14–17|cm|in|abbr=on}}, a mass of {{Convert|35–65|g|oz|abbr=on}}, and a [[wingspan]] of {{Convert|28–31|cm|in|abbr=on}}. It is similar to other falconets, but is the smallest by a small amount. Both sexes have glossy bluish-black {{Birdgloss|upperparts}}, black {{Birdgloss|flanks}} and outer thighs, and a black mask. The throat, cheeks, and breast are white, changing to pale yellowish-brown on the belly and {{Birdgloss|vent}}. The tail is short and black, with the wings reaching halfway to its tip. Males have a white {{Birdgloss|crown|forecrown}} and forehead, while females have this region reddish-brown. Females are also 9 per cent larger in size and have 4 per cent longer tails. In both sexes, the eyes are brown and the [[cere]] and legs are dark grey to black. Juveniles are similar to females, but have tawny-buff forecrowns and cheeks, while young females may also show reddish-brown on the forecrown.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Clark|first1=William S.|last2=Kirwan|first2=Guy M.|date=4 March 2020|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|title=White-fronted Falconet (''Microhierax latifrons'')|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/whffal1/1.0/introduction|language=en|publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]|doi=10.2173/bow.whffal1.01|url-access=subscription|access-date=13 February 2022|journal=Birds of the World|s2cid=241011146}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last1=Ferguson-Lees|first1=James|title=Raptors of the World|last2=Christie|first2=David|publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]]|others=Illustrated by Kim Franklin, David Mead, and Philip Burton|year=2001|isbn=978-1-4081-3550-1|location=London|pages=829–830|language=en|oclc=701718538}}</ref>

The species may be confused with the [[black-thighed falconet]], with which its range overlaps, but can be told apart from the latter and all other falconets by the colour of its forehead. The black-thighed falconet also has more intense reddish-brown on the belly and vent, along with white bars on the underside of the tail, which is pure black in the white-fronted falconet.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />

The white-fronted falconet's vocalizations are not well-known, but are assumed to be similar to those of other falconets.<ref name=":1" /> It makes a quavering ''kree-kree-kree''.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book|last=Myers|first=Susan|title=Birds of Borneo: Sabah, Sarawak, Brunei and Kalimantan|publisher=[[Christopher Helm Publishers|Christopher Helm]]|others=Illustrated by Richard Allen, Hilary Burn, Clive Byers, Daniel Cole, John Cox, Anthony Disley, Alan Harris, Szabolcs Kokay, Mike Langman, Ian Lewington, Andrew Mackay, Stephen Message, Christopher Schmidt, Jan Wilczur, and Tim Worfolk|year=2016|isbn=978-1-4729-2444-5|edition=Second|location=London|page=88|language=en|oclc=944318084}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The white-fronted falconet is [[endemic]] to Borneo, where it is found in the Malaysian states of [[Sarawak]] and [[Sabah]], along with the extreme northeastern part of [[Kalimantan]] (the Indonesian portion of Borneo).<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Irham|first1=Mohammad|last2=Meijaard|first2=Erik|last3=vas Balen|first3=Bas|date=2012|title=New information on the distribution of White-fronted ''Microhierax latifrons'' and Black-thighed Falconets ''M. fringillarius'' in Kalimantan, Indonesia|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/236856304|journal=[[Forktail (journal)|Forktail]]|volume=28|pages=162–163}}</ref> It inhabits open forests, [[forest edge]]s, clearings with dead trees, and cultivated regions with interspersed trees, and is thought to be especially common on mountainous slopes.<ref name=":1" /> It is also infrequently found in [[mangrove forest]]s.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last=Chye|first=Kim Lim|date=2012|title=Current status and distribution of diurnal raptors in Malaysia|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/258278480|journal=[[Ornis Mongolica]]|volume=1|pages=52–59}}</ref> The species is found at elevations of {{Convert|0–1200|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":1" />

== Behaviour and ecology ==
The white-fronted falconet is found alone or in groups of several individuals.<ref name=":1" /> The [[Generation time|generation length]] in the species is 3.2 years.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

The species mainly [[Insectivore|feeds on insects]] such as dragonflies, bees, and cicadas,<ref name=":4" /> but has also been reported chasing birds. Hunting parties have been observed in November.<ref name=":1" /> The species have been observed hunting from perches, making flights to catch flying insects and then eating the prey around two minutes after returning to the perch.<ref name=":4" /> Other hunting behaviours are thought to be similar to those of other falconets, although there may be differences in the hunting techniques of the present species and the black-thighed falconet.<ref name=":1" />

The breeding season of the white-fronted falconet is thought to be either from March to June or from March to April,<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> although breeding has also been observed in November.<ref name=":5" /> In Sabah, a male was seen feeding a fully-[[Fledge|fledged]] chick in late July.<ref name=":2" /> Nesting occurs in old [[Megalaimidae|barbet]] or [[woodpecker]] nest holes, with [[Clutch (eggs)|clutches]] comprising two eggs.<ref name=":5" /> The [[Egg incubation|incubation]] and fledging times are unknown.<ref name=":1" /> Adult males have been observed [[preening]] each other, but it is not known if it breeds communally like the [[collared falconet]].<ref name=":2" />

== Status ==
The white-fronted falconet is classified as being [[Near-threatened species|near threatened]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) on the [[IUCN Red List]], due to its small range, a moderately small population that is declining, and [[habitat loss]] within its range.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> It is also listed on [[Appendix II]] of [[CITES]].<ref name="cites status" /> The species' total population is estimated to be 15,000–30,000 total individuals, out of which 10,000–19,999 are adults. The population is estimated to be declining at a rate of 10–19% over a decade. Extensive [[deforestation]] in [[lowlands]] throughout its range, along with [[forest fires]], is a threat, although the presence of the species in areas of [[secondary growth]] shows a degree of resistance may imply that it is not immediately threatened. The [[cage-bird trade]] may also pose a threat to the species.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

zh.extract:
白額隼（學名：Microhierax latifrons）是猛禽類隼科的一種，也是最小型的猛禽，是婆羅洲沙巴地區的特有鳥類。
牠們長15-17cm，重35-65G，與麻雀的大小差不多。
牠們主要生活在低地森林和可耕地邊緣，以以蜻蜓、蜜蜂和蟬等昆蟲為主要獵物，牠們主要的威脅是棲息地消失。
繁殖季節是從3月到6月，儘管在11月也觀察到了繁殖。


參考
Phillipps, Quentin; & Phillipps, Karen (2011). Phillipps’ Field Guide to the Birds of Borneo. Oxford, UK: John Beaufoy Publishing. ISBN 978-1-906780-56-2.
